title: iPhone 17 Pro: In-Depth Camera Review
transcript: [music] This is the iPhone 17 Pro and it's the first iPhone that I think actually deserves pro in the name. Today, we're going to go in-depth on the cameras. There are some subtle upgrades this year that push this phone into professional territory. And even if you aren't a camera professional, I think there is something here for you to appreciate. This video is supported by Small Rig. Now, I am coming at this from the perspective of both a photographer and a cinematographer. If you're already up to date on the new hardware and software changes on the 17 Pro, I'll leave a time stamp here so you can skip straight into the evaluation of the cameras. For everyone else, let's just briefly go over what's new. Starting with the hardware differences, Apple introduced a brand new 48 megapixel 4x telephoto lens on the iPhone this year. This camera has a 56% larger sensor than previous generations, which should mean better lowlight performance and a slightly shallower depth of field to the right kind of shot. The lens itself works out to be a 100 millimeter with an f2.8 aperture. This is 20 mm shorter than last year's 16 Pro. There is also an 8x digital 200 mm that Apple markets as optical quality, but we'll see how that holds up a bit later. Surprisingly, one of the biggest innovations this year was in the selfie camera. Apple opted for a square sensor in this camera, which gives you an easy way to get either a vertical or horizontal aspect ratio for your shot without changing the orientation of your phone. They also added a feature called center stage that refframes the selfie camera based on how many people are detected in frame. In practice, I found the results of this to be a bit inconsistent, but it's also easy enough to turn off and just press the button to change the aspect ratio as needed. This is a huge convenience upgrade for this camera. If you're a creator, this new selfie camera paired with Apple Log might actually be a great way to quickly grab a direct to camera moment that you might want to use in a video later. There's also the new dual capture mode in the stock camera app where you can record both the selfie camera and any of the rear cameras at the same time. The downside is that it bakes this split screen view into one single clip, so you don't actually have the individual recordings to work with separately later. I'm not sure how widely useful this feature is, but I could see myself using it if I was surprising my kids with something and I wanted my reaction at the same time. Since I'm not typically an iPhone user, I don't have a set of AirPod Pros. However, it's worth mentioning that with iOS 26, you now have the ability to use AirPods as a microphone to record while shooting video. So, if you're in a pinch and you want to record audio while shooting on your phone, it's a great backup solution. I don't think this is going to replace a more pro mic setup, but it is definitely very convenient. Now, the most notable updates to the iPhone 17 Pro that I wanted to try out extensively are the additions of Apple Log 2 and the ability to record in ProRes RAW HQ. A simple explanation if you've never heard of either of these. Apple log 2 is Apple's new version of recording a flat image to the iPhone that allows more control of your colors in editing. Log recording is standard across basically every professional camera there is. ProRes RAW is the format that records unprocessed data directly from the iPhone sensor. Essentially, both of these serve as the way you can get complete control and color flexibility over how good the image will look coming from the iPhone. something that is kind of counter to Apple's philosophy of doing as much for the user upfront as possible. With that out of the way, let's start with the main 1x camera for video. Hardware wise, this main camera hasn't changed in quite a few generations, but I was still curious if the addition of Apple 2 and ProRes RAW make a noticeable difference. Let's look at a studio setup first. For this setup, I tested the 17 Pro against the 15 Pro that I had on hand. Settings were matched as close as possible with the ISO being one point different between the two in the Blackmagic camera app. Not sure why this is, but it's a negligible difference. The 15 Pro is shooting the original Apple log up to ProRes HQ, and the 17 Pro is shooting in Apple Log 2 up to ProRes RAW HQ. Looking at the 15 Pro's ProRes HQ versus the 17 Pro's ProRes RAW HQ footage, which is the highest quality each iPhone offers. The color difference between Apple Log and Apple Log 2 was immediately apparent. The 17 Pro shot looks more neutral and well balanced, whereas the 15 Pro shot skews a bit warmer overall. Something I also noticed with other test shots that we will look at shortly, but that you can also see here is that the ProRes HQ footage seems ever so slightly darker than the ProRes RAW footage. It's most noticeable in this plant in the back of this shot. This is subtle, but I was surprised to see this despite the settings being identical for both. Detail-wise, the ProRes RAW footage from the 17 Pro does seem sharper overall and when graded, looks to me more like what you get when you shoot with a proper cinema camera. Comparing ProRes HQ to ProRes HQ, we're seeing the same color differences, but to my eye, the 17 Pro again seems ever so slightly more detailed. In this instance, it's probably fairly difficult to spot. And given the hardware is basically the same, this difference must be something going on under the hood software- wise. Either way, the detail difference is negligible when you are shooting with the same codec from each iPhone. I also tested the 2x 48mm lens in ProRes HQ for each phone and came to the same conclusions as the other shots. If you're curious, here's how the ProRes RAW HQ compares to H.265. In a well-lit studio environment, both phones can look really good, but I will say that the 17 Pro does, at least in ProRes RAW HQ, come the closest I've ever seen an iPhone get to not looking like video from a smartphone. Moving on to this shot outdoors, the color difference between Apple log and Apple log 2 is fairly apparent. With just a basic recx 79 transform and no other adjustments, you can see the 17 Pro looks more nuanced in its colors. On the 15 Pro, my jacket looks bluish magenta, and on the 17 Pro, it just looks black. The entire 17 Pro shot looks and feels more accurate to what I saw in real life. Here's what these look like with some color grading. I think the best way I could describe the difference between the original Apple log and Apple log 2 is that Apple log 2 feels slightly cleaner in the way it handles colors. And this is due to the fact that Apple developed a new color space, Apple wide gamut. It has a wider color spectrum coverage over recre 2020, which is what the original Apple log uses. Here's where I was the most surprised though. In these low light shots of this bar, I was expecting the difference between the 15 Pro and the 17 Pro to be more drastic. To be honest, I found it pretty difficult in this situation to tell them apart. Given the wider color spectrum of Apple wide gamut, I thought a scene with highly saturated colors like this would show the differences more. I also did an instudio test with colored lights, and while you can see a color cast difference between the two shots. The 17 Pro again looked a bit cleaner in the blacks and shadows, the difference here wasn't huge. Even as I added saturation to push these to an extreme, I didn't notice any major breakage that made the 17 Pro drastically better. Overall, Apple Log 2 does seem to have more color information. This difference in the real world though doesn't feel huge, which isn't a bad thing, but it's just less of a difference than I expected. The inclusion of ProRes RAW also came with the ability to shoot 4K 4x3 OpenGate on the 17 Pro. OpenGate recording has been talked about a lot in camera videos for a little while now because it's a feature that even some professional cameras do not offer yet. While it's very cool that Apple supports OpenGate even in their first party Final Cut camera app, I personally think it's a bit of an overrated niche feature. The benefit is supposed to be that if you want to shoot video that you are going to deliver for both vertical and horizontal platforms, you only need to shoot it once for both. However, in my experience, in order to compose a shot that works for both vertical and horizontal delivery, you are compromising the composition for both deliverables. So, while it might take more time, I personally prefer to shoot for 16x9 or 9x6 instead of trying to do them at the same time. I know this isn't always possible, but I do this when I can. I think you will always get better looking footage this way because you don't have to compromise for multiple aspect ratios. This isn't to say that this feature isn't useful or doesn't have its place. It's possible you prefer to shoot open gate, in which case doing so on the iPhone is now possible and looks really good in ProRes RAW. However, despite the impressive image quality improvements ProRes RAW brings, before you go out and shoot absolutely everything in 4K OpenGate ProRes RAW, let's go over a few downsides. Once you activate ProRes RAW, the option to use Apple's 2x 48mm and 8x 200mm lens goes away. This is likely due to how resource intensive encoding ProRes RAW is. You even lose the hardware stabilization built into the cameras, which when you are using a camera as small as the iPhone, can make getting steady footage in ProRes RAW challenging. This is why I'm glad this video is supported by Small Rig. Small Rig just released their new iPhone 17 Pro ecosystem, and I used it extremely heavily throughout the production of this video. Here's a breakdown of the setup that I used. At the heart of the setup, we have Small Rig's dual handheld cage, which consists of a MagSafe phone case that has a built-in kickstand and this metal rail mount that surrounds the camera plateau that allows you to rig 67 mm filters to the setup or use a mount that works with external lenses like the ones from Moment. The case slots really nicely into the main cage, which lets you mount whatever accessories you want. This kit comes with two quick release handles that are super easy to attach and detach from the cage, and when you want, you can also rotate them to become a top handle when you need it. Each handle has a cold shoe mount that I use to mount my SSD and a small microphone. The left handle also comes with this tiny trigger remote so you can take a photo or hit record on your camera from up to 10 meters away to easily go from tripod to handheld shooting. They have this Arca-Swiss quick release that I use with their Hawklock quick release plate, but it also just works as is with any Arca compatible tripod. Because the setup adds a bit more weight and improves the points of contact on the iPhone, it makes shooting ProRes RAW handheld a lot more manageable. I also rigged a different setup so I could use the new Blackmagic Product so I have access to a more professional array of IO like full-size HDMI, a 3.5 millimeter audio input, and a type-C port for powering SSDs or other accessories. To keep this setup running all day, I use Small Rig's 50Wh Vmount with this MagSafe cheese plate attached, which keeps the setup fairly tidy, and I don't have to think about power while shooting. If you prefer to shoot super minimal, you can still attach the ND filter mount with enough clearance for even large SSDs like this Samsung T5 here. Small Rig also has more products that will be added to this ecosystem soon. So, if you want to be able to more easily shoot handheld or rig your iPhone basically anywhere, you can scan this QR code or check out the first link in the video description for a full breakdown of all the parts I used in my setup when making this video. The final con to consider when shooting with ProRes RAW HQ is the file sizes are pretty big. To put this in perspective, if you shot just 2 minutes a day of ProRes RAW HQ for an entire year, that would be nearly 5 terabytes of footage. Whereas, if it was H.265, 265, it would only be around 164 gigs. As much as I love shooting the highest quality possible with any camera that I use, it gets expensive quickly to even do a single professional project fully in ProRes RAW HQ. I think ProRes RAW is a decent middle ground since the data rates are less than ProRes 42:2 HQ. But either way, this is definitely a codec to use very intentionally and only when you need the extra color information. Even with the addition of ProRes RAW and Apple Log 2 this year, the iPhone does still have a pretty limited dynamic range that you can definitely feel with super bright highlights. Because of this, you are typically better off underexposing your iPhone footage by a stop or two depending on the scene just to make sure you are retaining as much of your highlight detail as possible. Because the iPhone can shoot at ISOs below 100, you retain a lot of information in your shadows, which makes bringing them up as needed, even in fairly low light shots, very doable. At the same time, this is still a smartphone, so don't expect it to see in the dark. That all being said, if you told me even a few years ago that the iPhone could produce video that looks as good as some of the stuff I've shot in ProRes RAW, I'm not sure that I would have believed you. iPhone footage can now be cut with professional cinema cameras very easily. title: iPhone 13 Pro vs. iPhone 17 Pro: Lohnt sich das Upgrade?
transcript: Three weeks ago I switched from an iPhone 13 Pro to an iPhone 17 Pro and compared the two smartphones in detail.  This video will show you in which areas they differ significantly and where they are more similar than you might think. I test all the important aspects like the cameras, performance, and battery life, and then I'll tell you which iPhone I would currently recommend.  I hope you have lots of fun.  Let's go. I am currently giving away 42 of Trackard's rechargeable Airtch cards, which can even ring.  The competition runs until October 31, 2025, and to participate, simply write a comment below this video. Subscribing to my channel and liking the video is also not a bad idea.  Also, feel free to check out Tracker's website, which I've linked in the info box.  And now we begin.  The iPhone 17 Pro comes with a significantly redesigned look that is clearly different from previous generations. While the 13 Pro and the 14 Pro still use a stainless steel frame, Apple uses an aluminum unibody design for the first time in the 17 Pro .  Opinions definitely differ on this. Some find the combination of the thermally conductive aluminum and the integrated vapor chamber ingenious. Others want the more scratch-resistant titanium from the 15 and 16 Pro back, which was only used in the iPhone Air this year.  The front of the 17 Pro is made of the new Ceramic Shield 2, which is significantly more shatter-resistant and scratch-resistant than the Ceramic Shield 1 found in the 13 Pro as well as the 14, 15 and 16 Pro.  At around 206g, the iPhone 17 Pro is also only 2g heavier than the 13 Pro.  Visually, the most noticeable feature is the redesigned camera module , which now protrudes more prominently from the housing.  In terms of color, Apple currently only offers three variants: Silver, Cosmic Orange and Deep Blue, while the 13 Pro was available in Sierra Blue and Silver as well as classic colors like Graffit and Gold.  Although both iPhones are almost the same size, the 17 Pro offers slightly more screen area at 6.3 inches thanks to its thinner display bezels compared to the 13 Pro at 6.1 inches.  The increased brightness is also noticeable .  While the 13 Pro reaches up to 1200 nits, the 17 Pro reaches up to 3000 nits outdoors, making content significantly easier to read in direct sunlight. 1200 nits is also quite bright, and even here you rarely have problems seeing everything clearly.  The iPhone 13 Pro was the first iPhone to feature ProMotion, an adaptive refresh rate of 1 to 120 Hz. The iPhone 17 Pro also has ProMotion and, thanks to Ceramic Shield 2, an anti-reflective coating that makes a difference in some situations compared to previous generations , although not a huge one.  Even though the display of the iPhone 17 Pro is superior here, the differences are not as huge as the price difference might suggest .  Let's take a look at the cameras now. The camera system of the iPhone 17 Pro surpasses the already powerful setup of the iPhone 13 Pro in almost every area.  While the 13 Pro still relies on 3 12 megapixel sensors for wide-angle, ultra-wide-angle and telephoto lenses, the 17 Pro features a new Fusion system in which all three lenses are equipped with 48 megapixel sensors. The main camera sensor on the 17 Pro is slightly larger, which results in even better image quality.  The sensor in the 13 Pro is definitely a solid size for a smartphone.  Both smartphones take decent pictures, although the 17 Pro's are even more detailed thanks to its 48 megapixels.   It becomes especially clear when you zoom in on the images. The difference is also there with the ultra-wide-angle camera, but at first glance it's really only minimal.  Only when you zoom in a bit here do more captured details become visible in the iPhone 17 Pro images. The difference is particularly noticeable with the telephoto zoom.  The iPhone 13 Pro offers triple optical zoom, while the iPhone 17 Pro features a new telephoto lens with quadruple optical zoom.  Additionally, thanks to its 48-megapixel sensor, the iPhone 17 Pro can achieve a so-called lossless zoom of up to eight times by cropping into the central area of ​​the sensor .  Furthermore, the sensor size of the telephoto camera has increased considerably compared to the 16 Pro.  The front camera has also been redesigned.  Instead of 12 megapixels like in the 13 Pro, the 17 Pro now uses a square 18 megapixel sensor with Center Stage function. Furthermore, photos and videos can be taken in landscape format, resulting in a significantly wider-angle image, which is particularly advantageous for selfies with multiple people.  In terms of image quality, the selfies on the 17 Pro are slightly sharper, but even here it's not a huge difference. In the video area, the 17 Pro offers a clear advantage.  Compared to the iPhone 13 Pro, the iPhone 17 Pro supports Lock recording with Apple Logless RAW Openate Genlock and Time Code synchronization features that were previously reserved for professional film cameras .  The video quality from the iPhone 17 Pro is truly incredibly good.  But don't get me wrong, the 13 Pro also takes pretty good videos for a smartphone, but side by side one simply looks much more like a smartphone and the other much more like a professional cinema camera .  To achieve such a look with the be 17 Pro , you often have to spend some time in post-processing .  I also produced a detailed camera review for the iPhone 17 Pro.  I'll definitely link it in the info box.   Feel free to take a look .  And now let's continue with the performance. Thanks to its aluminum unibody design and integrated vapor chamber cooling, the iPhone 17 Pro can dissipate heat more efficiently .  This means that the performance remains more stable even during longer periods of stress than with its predecessors, which tend to start throttling performance earlier under continuous load. While the 13 Pro is still powered by the A15 Bionic , which was manufactured using a 5 nanometer process, Apple uses the new A19 Pro in the 17 Pro, which, like the A18 Pro, is based on a 3 nanometer architecture. Single-core performance has increased by over 50% compared to the 13 Pro, and multi-core performance by over 60%.  Graphics performance has also been noticeably improved.  If we compare the benchmark results of the 17 Pro with the results of the 13 Pro, we see an increase of approximately 125%. This is particularly noticeable in demanding tasks such as gaming, professional video editing, filmmaking with the new professional camera features, or 3D calculations.  Nevertheless, the iPhone 13 Pro still runs super smoothly and is very convenient to use in everyday life.  Apps open faster, in milliseconds, and if there are any stutters, it's usually due to the still buggy iOS 26 and less to the lower peak performance.  So, in terms of performance and especially considering the price-performance ratio, you definitely can't go wrong with the iPhone 13 Pro, even in 2025 .  However, if you really need a lot of performance but don't want to spend quite so much, I recommend looking at a refurbished iPhone 16 Pro, which also has a chip with 3 nanometer architecture and also has very strong performance in both the CPU and GPU areas.  And as just mentioned, let's briefly talk about iOS 26. Opinions on this are at least as divided as they are on the design of the 17 Pro.  The new Liquid Glass look makes everything appear a bit more transparent and smoother.  While this is aesthetically pleasing, it also means that, for example, text is sometimes harder to read and the reflection makes it appear slightly less minimalist .  Furthermore, there are, for example, new lock screen interfaces with dynamic home position and a revised camera app with changed switches. Both smartphones are also IP68 certified, which means they are protected against dust and immersion for up to 30 minutes. Unlike the iPhone 13 Pro, the iPhone 17 Pro features Apple Intelligence, Apple's integrated AI system. In the long term, Apple Intelligence aims to turn the iPhone into a personal assistant that, for example, takes over tasks such as summarizing texts or documents and provides personalized suggestions.  Many functions are also intended to run directly on the device to protect privacy. For many, however, this doesn't play a huge role in their purchase decision for the iPhone 17 Pro, as many of the features are not yet fully available or only usable to a limited extent, if at all.  So, these are the microphones of the two iPhones. You can see if you can notice a difference.  This , as I said, is the iPhone 13 Pro and this is the iPhone 17 Pro.  Exactly.  And now we'll continue with the battery.  While the iPhone 13 Pro manages with a battery of around 3100 mAh, the iPhone 17 Pro has approximately 4000 mAh.  This increase, combined with the more efficient A19 Pro chip and optimized energy management through better heat dissipation, results in a noticeably longer battery life in everyday use.  According to Apple, the iPhone 17 Pro achieves a local video playback time of up to 31 hours, while the 13 Pro manages about 22 hours under the same conditions .  Even in more demanding usage scenarios, such as streaming or high display brightness, the new model lasts noticeably longer. Both iPhones can be charged wirelessly via Maxave or a charging pad. Here, the iPhone 13 Pro achieves up to 15 watts and the iPhone 17 Pro up to 25 watts charging power.  Using a cable, the iPhone 17 Pro can reach up to 50% charge in about 20 minutes.  Provided that a high-performance power supply is used. The iPhone 13 Pro reaches 50% in about 30 minutes.  This one also uses the Lightning connector, while the iPhone 17 Pro uses USB-C.  When buying a refurbished iPhone 13 Pro, you should make sure that the battery is not too worn out.  My recommendation would be at least 85%, even better would be 90% maximum capacity in terms of battery health.  Conclusion: The iPhone 13 Pro is still a very powerful device.  Even today, a refurbished model for under €400 can provide a smartphone that offers solid quality in many areas.  It boasts strong performance, good camera quality, and is the first iPhone with a Pro Motion display.  The iPhone 17 Pro, however, offers noticeable improvements with a powerful A19 Pro chip with 3 nanometer architecture, an improved camera system with professional video features, an improved battery, and the new aluminum unibody design with Ceramic Shield 2 and better heat dissipation.  If you're looking for a sweet spot, you could take a look at the iPhone 15 Pro, which, although it has a chip with 5 nanometer architecture, still has Apple Intelligence and is generally very close to the iPhone 16 Pro.  Feel free to write in the comments which iPhone you would currently buy and what you think of the iPhone 17 Pro .  Please give the video a thumbs up if you liked it.  Subscribe to my channel if you're interested in videos like this. That would make me incredibly happy.  And then I wish you a nice day.  Goodbye, my dears.  Until then, peace.

title: iPhone 17 Pro vs iPhone Air – Der ultimative Vergleich im Alltagstest!
transcript: I wish you a wonderful evening and welcome you to my latest video.  A few weeks ago I already presented my final review of the iPhone 17 Pro and also of the brand new iPhone Air.  These are two models that, price-wise (assuming we always refer to the entry-level model), are located in roughly the same region, but nevertheless appeal to different target groups and also offer different hardware.  For all of you who do n't know at the moment whether you 're still a Pro or possibly will be this year, I made this video to show you a comparison between the iPhone Air, an ultra-thin smartphone, and the iPhone Pro, the most powerful smartphone Apple currently offers.  If you'd like to know how this comparison turns out, then please stay tuned.  I wish you much fun. Although I don't want to talk too much about the technical specifications, I would still like to consider the dimensions and weight, as the Air model in particular is one of the thinnest smartphones on the market .  For a quick comparison, the iPhone Air is larger than the regular iPhone 17 Pro.  It is also wider, but at approximately 3.1 mm it is also significantly thinner.  So we're only talking about 5.6 mm here.  Another absolutely outstanding feature of the iPhone Air is its incredibly low weight of 165g.  The iPhone 17 Pro weighs 206g, which is mainly due to significantly fewer components and a much thinner battery .  In its latest generation, Apple's processor no longer uses titanium, as was the case with the 15-inch and 16-inch versions, but now uses recycled aluminum.  So, aluminum for the iPhone 17 Pro, but a titanium casing for the iPhone Air, with both devices being protected at least on the front by Seramic Shield 2 . Due to its design, the iPhone Air also has this feature on the back.  I just briefly mentioned the dimensions. The iPhone Air is wider, it is taller, it has a larger display, but to me it actually feels significantly more premium than the aluminum casing of the iPhone 17 Pro.  This is n't just due to the combination of titanium and glass, but primarily because the aluminum casing scratches very easily, not only for me but for many other users as well, and also feels somewhat hollow and significantly less premium on the back than the iPhone Air. Therefore, if we're solely concerned with material choice and perceived quality, the iPhone Air is the clear winner.  Those who prefer a slightly more compact device are better off with the iPhone 17 Pro. Both models are also IP68 certified, which means they are conditionally protected against water ingress and completely protected against dust ingress.  The Air not only feels absolutely premium due to this choice of materials, but also has that wow factor that I, or rather you, have been missing from Apple for years .  Because not only is it incredibly thin, it also looks sexy, it fits well in the hand, and it has super thin bezels around the display on the front.  Incredibly, both models use Face ID for unlocking, the 3D face unlock that Apple has been offering for years, and it actually works perfectly on both models. However, Apple takes a different approach to the speaker setup, particularly on the iPhone Air, where only a mono speaker is used above the front camera.  The iPhone 17 Pro also features a speaker in the base of the device, which this year has been moved to the left side of the USB-C port. Both of these setups definitely sound good.  The iPhone 17 Pro, due to its two speakers, is also significantly more spacious, and due to the thickness of the casing, also significantly bulkier.  They sound more nuanced and, in my opinion, are 30 to 40% better than those of the iPhone Air. Which is a bit of a shame, since the iPhone Air, with its larger display, is actually perfectly suited for multimedia consumption.  Regarding the displays, I already briefly mentioned it.  6.3 inches in the iPhone 17 Pro, 6.5 inches in the iPhone Air, both with a maximum refresh rate, i.e., how smoothly the image can be displayed, up to 120 Hz and a maximum brightness of 3000 nits in automatic mode.  Everything related to color accuracy, brightness, viewing angle stability, and so on is at an identical and really good level, so both models actually get a point here.  Let's move on to the next two points: performance, and consequently, of course, heat generation and, above all, battery life.  Both models use the A19 Pro chip .  However, the A19 Pro in the iPhone Air, and of course the graphics unit as well, are somewhat less powerful, and theoretically the iPhone Air, especially when under load, will reach its limits more quickly.  Of course, all of this can also be done in various stress tests, and that is just as understandable.  However, I can also tell you that in everyday use, and this will affect 99.5% of all users, it will not be noticeable at all , as it will still be among the most powerful iPhones on the market .  Under full load, I recorded a heat development of approximately 43°C in the camera area of ​​the iPhone Air .  As mentioned, the iPhone 17 Pro uses the slightly more powerful A19 Pro chip .  We have very good heat dissipation here, for which the aluminum housing is of course ideally suited, and under full load I suddenly reached 38°C.  This also clearly demonstrates how well the thermals work and that the Pro model is definitely intended for more performance-oriented users.  Regarding battery life, I got roughly identical results for both models.  Yes, the iPhone 17R has a significantly smaller battery, but the processor is also somewhat less powerful. I had at least 4.5 to 5 hours of screen-on time here .  With the iPhone 17 Pro, it was usually five hours, sometimes even 5.5 hours, although on both devices I always had automatic brightness control set or between 50 and 60% of the maximum brightness .  In the evenings, around 9 pm, I usually still had about 20 to 25% remaining battery capacity in both devices.  When I simply ran everything over Wi-Fi, watched Netflix, manually adjusted the brightness and volume to 50% , I achieved video playback times of between 15 and 16 hours on both devices .  Therefore, regarding performance, and especially battery and charging, I am extremely satisfied with both models, and I am also absolutely surprised by the iPhone Air, so that I would actually award the point to the iPhone Air , since it has a significantly smaller battery.   Let 's talk about the camera, which is actually one of the most important aspects of smartphones these days, and of course also of Apple devices, and therefore also of both models here.  We have one thing in common on the front: the 18 megapixel front camera.  Otherwise, we have a completely different setup, namely the iPhone Air only has one camera.  This is a 48 megapixel main camera, whereas with the Proas we have a 48 megapixel main camera, a corresponding telephoto camera and another 48 megapixel ultra wide-angle camera.  Anyone who is already at the point where they say, " I need macro photography in a smartphone, or ultra- wide-angle photography in a smartphone, or good telephoto capabilities, meaning to bring objects closer, be it building structures in urban areas or even the birds here in autumn in the thorn hedge in the countryside."  He can skip this chapter right now and should go for the iPhone 17 Pro, because the iPhone is unlikely to be able to do all that, or at least not with sufficient power.  There is no macro photography or macro camera in the car, no ultra-wide-angle camera, which is usually also associated with it.  There is a 2x telephoto zoom, but that's a digital crop.  This means that by digitally zooming into the image, this doubled quality is achieved; with the iPhone 17 Pro, up to eight times zoom is possible, and accordingly, up to eight times better quality is achieved.  Furthermore , as mentioned, there is the ultra-wide-angle camera, which is currently among the best on the market, and also a main camera that reproduces very natural photos, just like the one on the iPhone Air, which is also great. Therefore, if we compare the two cameras, the iPhone 17 Pro and the Air, I'm talking about the 48 megapixel main camera here, then you will actually find very good, sometimes even very identical, pictures on both devices. That means the main camera in the iPhone Air is really very good, too. However, I found the front camera on both devices to be very, very similar.  That means I had good color reproduction and a good level of detail.  For me, the images on both smartphones were a bit too soft, which is n't noticeable when comparing them directly, but especially when compared to other devices, such as the Pixel 10 Pro.  They are good to very good, I would say, but in my opinion not as good as on a Samsung smartphone or even the Pixel 10 Pro.  However, the video quality is identically good on both smartphones at 4K and 60 frames per second, so very good even with the iPhone 17 Pro perhaps with slight advantages. The 17 Pro offers significantly greater advantages , especially in that it allows you to record video in Pro mode and also to take photos in RAW, which the iPhone simply doesn't offer.  This is where the difference becomes most apparent.  The ProDell is designed for the professional user.  The Air model is more for users who want a stylish smartphone. I don't really need to elaborate on the software, because one thing should be clear: both devices use iOS 26 .  And anyone who has seen my videos about the iPhone Pro or the iPhone 17 Pro will have noticed that I haven't always spoken positively about them, because for me these iOS 26 versions are the most unfinished versions that Apple has ever released for a new device.  I need to harp on this point again, namely iOS 26 at the current time, because I recently read in comments that other manufacturers aren't any better.  However, I cannot actually recall that any manufacturer selling these kinds of premium devices for over €1200 has ever included as many bugs or crashes in the operating system in the last 5 years as Apple has with the latest iPhone generation.  And I hear that again and again from my circle of friends.   One feature that is definitely worth mentioning is Apple Intelligence, Apple's own AI assistant, which can be activated by holding down the power button and which delivers really great results, especially in collaboration with ChatGPT .  This has been really well solved, although, for example, photo editing on Apple devices doesn't work as well as on Google smartphones or especially on Samsung smartphones.  That brings us to my brief conclusion. Which models are suitable for whom?  The iPhone 17R, no, it's only called the iPhone Air if you're looking for a lightweight and extremely thin device, need a large bright display, but do n't necessarily need the zoom, ultrawide, ultrawide angle, or macro monster in the camera, and the everyday performance of the scaled-down CPU and GPU is definitely sufficient.  Another negative point , at least in my opinion, is the single mono speaker above the display, as it simply cannot convey this spatial sound as well as, for example, the Filter 17 Pro.  The Pro model is really for you if you want maximum performance, better thermals, and of course, a better camera.   In my opinion, both are relatively equal when it comes to displays. However, in my opinion, even the Pro model uses the inferior material, or rather this mix, namely the aluminum combination with Seramic Shield, which makes titanium my first choice for the iPhone Air .  In summary, if you 're looking for a smartphone that's super high-quality and has a Wowtor camera, and you only rarely use the camera, for example to quickly take a snapshot, then the iPhone is definitely the right choice for you.  However, if you say you're a hardcore gamer, you take a lot of photos and you really enjoy doing so, then the iPhone 17 Pro is definitely the better choice for you.  And that's exactly where I end up.  Thank you for watching.  I would be happy if you enjoyed the video and of course if you tune in to future videos.  Until then, take care and bye.

It reminds me a lot of what happened with the camera control button on the iPhone 16, that is, an idea that is there, but that in day-to-day life, at least for me, does not provide real value.  Then there's the issue of the screen, and here I was a little disappointed, to be honest.  Apple talked a lot about this new screen, well, it had an improved anti-reflective coating and, well, I was really looking forward to seeing it, I mean, I imagined a screen that would look perfect outdoors without having to look for shade.  I even wondered if it was worth putting a protector on it this year in case it took away that new property.  And it's true that outdoors the screen looks superb, but when you're indoors with spotlights, etc., you can still see the reflections and there isn't that much difference.  I mean, it wasn't that big of a deal.  As soon as I could, I put the iPhone 16 Pro Max next to the iPhone 17 Pro and, well, there is a slight improvement, I won't deny it, but it's nowhere near the revolution I was expecting, I mean, it's still on par with what Samsung achieves, for example, with its screens.  In the end, I just put a screen protector on it as usual and forgot about it, which, well, is a shame, but if I have to be honest, what I've missed the most is not something the iPhone does, but something it doesn't do.  In other words, I'm referring to the lack of new software that takes advantage of everything it has inside.  It's incredibly powerful hardware, but you encounter limitations that don't make much sense these days.  For example, action mode, which is great for stabilization, still can't record 4K video, and 8K video recording is still unavailable.  And look, I know this is a very niche thing, but for those of us who create content, having that extra resolution to be able to crop in editing without losing quality is a very valuable tool.  And above all, what weighs most heavily on him is the feeling that the software has fallen behind, that is, the absence of truly new artificial intelligence features that make this iPhone feel smarter, more useful.  In the end, you're left with a strange aftertaste because after the first few weeks the excitement fades, and apart from those good details I mentioned earlier, the daily experience feels very, very similar to last year's.  And I think that's the real problem, one that goes beyond this iPhone.  I feel, and this is a very personal opinion, that the hardware in smartphones has ceased to inspire.  I mean, think about it for a second.  Do you remember the excitement from years ago?  Today that magic has been lost.  And be aware, because this isn't a problem exclusive to Apple, and certainly not for someone who bought a high-end Samsung Galaxy last year. What real improvement have they noticed compared to this year's model, at the very least?  Or the one who had a Google Pixel 9 Pro. I bought one myself.  And well, anyone who has seen the Google Pixel 10 Pro has felt a revolution with this new generation.  Well, not really. It seems we've reached a point where improvements are incremental, small percentages here and there, a little more brightness, a little more zoom, a slightly faster chip, but the party of big innovations, the ones that could really excite us, seems to be over.  So, where is the next frontier?  Where is the magic now?  For me it's crystal clear, it's in here, it's in the software and more specifically in the artificial intelligence that lives within it. The next big leap won't be a phone that folds in a new way; it will be an assistant that anticipates your needs, software that truly makes your life easier, more productive, and more creative. And this is where, paradoxically, Apple seems to be having the most problems.  That major Siri update, which promised to revolutionize everything, has had to be delayed.  Or we see how, for example, Apple Intelligence is progressing at a much slower pace than we all expected, while, for example, we see the competition stepping on the gas.  And that's the feeling I'm left with after a month with the iPhone 17 Pro Max.  I have in my hands an absolutely incredible piece of engineering, almost perfect hardware, but it feels underutilized.  In short, perhaps the future lies not so much in the device we carry in our pocket, but in the intelligence that connects them all.  But that's another story.   I'd love to know what you all think about all this.  Do you think hardware still has room to surprise us, or do you agree that the real game is now being played in software and artificial intelligence?  Leave it in the comments, I'm very curious to read them.  And if you're still hungry for more, here are some other recommended videos, including one about Apple's problems with artificial intelligence that we uploaded in the middle of this week.  Don't miss it, seriously.  See you tomorrow with more content here at the bitten apple, because you know, I'm Fernando and I promise to do it.

Whichever one appeals to you more , both will receive long-term software support.  Google provides support for its Pixel 10 Pro for 7 years.  Apple's average lifespan is 6 to 7 years, but there's no definitive answer. Both smartphones have received a larger battery compared to their predecessors .  But the biggest leap was actually made by the iPhone. While the Pixel 10 Pro increases by 170 mAh to a total of 4870 mAh , the iPhone 17 Pro gains over 400 mAh, reaching almost 4000 mAh. But wait a second, that's over 20% less.  How can the iPhone compete with that?  The answer: A very efficient chip and excellent adaptation of the software to the hardware. And that's why the iPhone boasts good battery life, as I average 5.5 to 1.5 hours of screen time in everyday use.  The Pixel 10 Pro, on average, lasts for four and a half to five and a half hours.  Both will get you through a day just fine.  Neither of them are real battery monsters, though .  For that, you'd need something like the Pixel Pro XL or iPhone Pro Max. In the Android world, the Pixel 10 Pro achieves a PC battery benchmark that, unfortunately, does n't exist for iPhones.  At just under 13 and a half hours, however, that's a really good result.   This means it lasts longer than a Samsung S25 Plus, but it doesn't come close to a Xiaomi 15. Apple and Google are very similar when it comes to charging.  While the Pixel cable is limited to up to 30 watts, the iPhone draws up to 35 watts and is unusually fast.  This means both devices are fully charged in approximately 80 to 100 minutes , which isn't exactly super fast. But there is some good news regarding wireless charging.  Pixel Snap finally brings magnets to G2 charging.  Just like with the iPhone and Maxf, you can now attach a magnetic puck, a power bank, or other accessories to the back.  And yes, Maxf gadgets are also compatible with the Pixel .  While the iPhone already uses up to 25 watts of LED power, the Pixel only offers 15 watts. Both smartphones come with a triple camera including a periscope telescope, so they have very similar features in this respect as well.  The Pixel features a 50 megapixel main camera, a 48 megapixel ultra-wide-angle camera, and a 48 megapixel telephoto camera with five times optical zoom.  The iPhone features a 48- megapixel main camera, a 48-megapixel ultra-wide camera, and a 48- megapixel telephoto camera with four times optical zoom.  Which of the two takes the better photos?  In daylight, both smartphones deliver very strong results, although with some differences in characteristics.  The iPhone uses a slightly warmer color scheme.  With this classic car, the Apple smartphone can therefore capture the turquoise side more accurately.  The pixel is slightly off here.  This delicious pizza looks even more delicious on the Pixel .  Don't you think so too?  Why the pixel depicts the yellow autumn landscape as if it were currently an ice age is a mystery to me.  Thankfully, that remained the exception.  The iPhone delivers slightly crisper images thanks to its standard 24-megapixel camera .  The difference is only noticeable upon close inspection. However, the close focusing distance is clearly in favor of the Pixel.  It focuses from about 12 cm, while the iPhone only focuses from almost 17 cm .  After that, the iPhone automatically activates macro mode via the ultra-wide camera, which doesn't always look high-quality.  Nevertheless, the ultra-wide-angle cameras score points with their good quality.  Both deliver sharp, unforgiving images with similar color and detail quality. Surprisingly often, the results are almost identical.  In the opposite direction, both smartphones impress with a high-quality 2x zoom through the main camera. The optical zoom via the periscope cameras also delivers sharp and detailed results. The Pixel tends to produce brighter zoomed-in photos, while the iPhone captures slightly more fine detail.  When digitally magnified, it becomes apparent that the eight-fold hybrid image of the iPhone sometimes appears clearer than the ten-fold pixel image. However, the situation changes at around 30x magnification .  The Pixel scores points here with its AI- based Super Resolution Zoom, which retains a surprising amount of detail, especially at 100x zoom, although not always perfectly.  On the iPhone, however, the digital zoom stops at 40x.  For night shots, I prefer the Pixel slightly, as it makes the scene appear brighter overall and brings out more detail from dark areas .  The iPhone, on the other hand, captures the lighting mood in a slightly more atmospheric way.  I am impressed by the macro mode on both the Pixel and the iPhone.  Close-up shots from both artists are exciting and detailed. However, I particularly prefer the pixelated look for flowers, as the texture is finer and the colors are more natural. Regarding portraits, both provide a clean separation between subject and background sharpness.  Personally, I prefer the portraits on the Pixel, even though the iPhone looks more natural with a softer transition at the edges .  In terms of video quality, both are top-level.  The Pixel does n't need to hide from the iPhone here.  The zoom function is smooth and the stabilization is pleasantly gentle. However, they differ significantly in color.  Technically, the Pixel offers a higher level of detail with 8K videos, while the iPhone, with its professional Pro RAW format, is clearly aimed at movies.  Finally, a word about selfies.  Here, the iPhone can of course stand out due to its almost square sensor, which makes it possible to take photos in portrait and landscape format without rotating the smartphone. Compared to the Pixel, iPhone selfies appear somewhat softer and not quite as crisp. In the end, we have two really strong compact smartphones here that have hardly any weaknesses.  High-quality, IP68-rated water-resistant design with super bright 120 Hz OLEDs, powerful triple camera, good battery life and magnetic wireless charging.  The iPhone 17 Pro scores points with its enormous performance, longer battery life despite a smaller battery, and the usual close interconnectivity within the Apple ecosystem.  I personally really like the new design, especially here in orange. For creators, Proest RA Video is a particularly strong argument, and in general, the iPhone is a really good phone for filming.  I also think the flexible front camera with its almost square sensor is really clever. The maximum focus distance of the main camera could be improved, the full-screen brightness is somewhat lower, storage upgrades are extremely expensive, and iOS 26 is still struggling with minor bugs, not to mention the current, still limited, integration of AI features. In comparison, the Pixel 10 Pro features a super bright display and a significantly better focus limit for the main camera.  But in my opinion, its greatest strength is the software. Clean Android 16 with countless useful AI features such as Gemini Live, Live Call Translation or Circle to Search.  8K video capabilities and a good, though not outstanding, battery life are also advantages .  The Tensor G5 chip used may not be in the top league in terms of performance, but it is fast enough for everyday use.  Criticism is leveled at the camera's sometimes inconsistent color calibration and the sluggish UFS 3.1 storage in the base version.  Incomprehensible for a flagship model in this price range. Ultimately, both could be a little faster when charging , but all in all, these are two extremely sophisticated smartphones, each playing to its own strengths .  The iPhone with its performance and ecosystem, the Pixel with its clever AI features.  For true battery monsters, you should opt for the larger Pixel 10 Pro XL or iPhone 17 Pro Max. The Pixel 10 Pro is already available for under €950.  However, I recommend the 256 GB version starting at €1050.  The iPhone 17 Pro, on the other hand, is close to its RRP of €1299.  Let's see when and if it gets cheaper.  Which of the compact Pros would you ultimately choose?   Feel free to write it in the comments.  I'm curious to hear your opinions.  Tune in again soon. It's good.  See you later.  Ciao.

title: #tepukkartu
transcript: What you're about to see will blow your mind. The moment the iPhone 17 Pro Max dropped, this guy got so hyped that he literally jumped straight into a [music] swimming pool with the phone in his hand. Everyone thought he'd lost his mind, but then he started unboxing it underwater, pulled out the charger, and even tried to plug it in while still in the pool. And here's the craziest part. The phone actually looked like it was charging for a few >> [music]

title: iPhone 17 Pro: революция или эволюция?
transcript: [music] Hello.  This is the iPhone 17 Pro.  Perhaps the most unusual and at the same time most controversial iPhone of recent years.  He divided all fans into two camps. those who consider it a breakthrough, and those who, on the contrary, see it as a kind of simplification and deviation from the canons.  I've been using the smartphone for almost two weeks now, and in this video I'll break down what's truly cool about it, and what, let's say, has raised questions and heated debates.  So get comfortable and let's get started.  And I think it will be interesting.  And thanks to our friends from Srink for the opportunity to test the new iPhone.  They already have the entire iPhone 17 lineup in stock.  You can choose any model, order and pick it up on the same day.  In addition to smartphones, there are computers, laptops, televisions, home and kitchen appliances, and a wide variety of gadgets.  Please note that all products on the website are original and come with an official warranty, making it safe and secure to buy .  Plus, there are discounts and bonuses for regular customers, so shopping there is also profitable.  I'll leave the link in the description.  Go ahead and take a look.  and I'm sure you'll find something interesting for yourself.   The first and perhaps most significant innovation in the iPhone 17 Pro is, of course, the design.  Instead of the previous design of glass and titanium, we now have a seamless, all-aluminum body.  It is machined from a single block of metal, which means there are no joints between the parts at all.  To the touch it feels like an absolutely monolithic construction, strong, integral and durable.   The smartphone arrived to me in the most hyped orange color.  And I'll be honest, I had mixed feelings about this shade at first.  In promotional renders and official photographs, the color looked rich, stylish, and bright.  However, in person it seemed more muted to me and somewhat reminded me of the palette of some Chinese brands.  It's hard to say whether this is due to the frame being painted to match the case or the scale of the changes overall .  Either way, getting used to the iPhone's new look and truly appreciating it will definitely take time.   It took me a couple of days to do this, but then the old design, on the contrary, began to seem somewhat archaic.  And in fact, this feeling is not without foundation, since the last time Apple carried out such a large-scale redesign was back in the twelfth generation of its smartphones, that is, 5 years ago. However, there are some small questions regarding the practicality of the seventeenth iPhone .  Anaplated aluminum is clearly not the most durable material.  After a couple of weeks of very careful testing, I managed to catch a couple of micro-scratches on it. So a case for this model is an absolutely necessary thing, at least if you plan to keep it in good condition for a long time . Now let's talk about the display.  In essence, almost nothing has changed in it.  In front of us is a magnificent 120Hz aleto screen of the highest quality.  The only claimed improvement is peak brightness, which has increased from 2 to 3,000 nits.  It sounds impressive, of course, but in practice you are unlikely to notice the difference. The screen can only produce such brightness for a very short time and only in certain scenarios, for example, when exposed to direct sunlight.  In normal everyday use, brightness remains roughly at the level of the previous generation.  Personally, I did n't notice any difference.  The phone also boasts a new anti-reflective coating.  But again, whether this is an advantage or not is debatable.  There seems to be a coating, but it works worse than, for example, on Samsung flagships.  But what really pleased me was the appearance of DCMming.  Now in the settings you can completely disable pulsed brightness modulation, which we call PWM.  This should reduce screen flickering and reduce eye strain when using your smartphone in the dark. It's true that there's one important caveat to note: not all users are sensitive to PWM.  And personally, I, for example, never noticed any discomfort from screen flickering.  But for those who experience eye strain or headaches from PWM, this is really good news.  The new iPhone won't have this problem.  You can safely use your phone even at night.  As for the elements on the case, they have not changed at all in the new generation .  On the right side there is a power button and a touch-sensitive camera button, and on the left side there is a volume control and econ key.  If you are already accustomed to this layout, no surprises await you.  Difficulties may arise only for owners of Android devices.   It will take them some time to adapt.  The speakers are here and they are very cool.  Apple, as always, is at its best in this regard .  The sound is rich and detailed, you can hear every little detail.  Despite the fact that we are dealing with a smartphone, there is bass, but wheezing, on the contrary, does not appear even at maximum volume.  So, this smartphone gets a solid A for its sound .  In the camera module, changes affected only the telephoto lens.  The iPhone 17 Pro received a completely new 48-megapixel telephoto lens with an F2.8 aperture instead of the previous 12-megapixel one. It can take photos with 4x optical zoom. As expected, the detail in these photos has increased slightly, but I can’t say the difference is colossal.  iPhones used to take very good photos with telephoto lenses. But thanks to the increased resolution, you can now take photos with eightfold digital zoom.  True, they only look good in ideal lighting conditions.  When there is not enough light, detail is reduced and noise begins to appear.  However, this is a completely normal situation for digital zoom .   The base and ultra-wide lenses retain the same 48-megapixel sensors.   The main camera with an F1.8 aperture is ideal for portraits with artistic background blur, and the ultra-wide camera with F2.8 is perfect for shooting landscapes and architecture.  I would like to point out that both lenses shoot like flagships. I liked the basic camera's detail, wide dynamic range, and accurate color rendition.  And the ultra-wide lens has sharpness across the entire frame.  As you can see, there is no loss of detail at all towards the edges of the images .  I also liked the night shots .  There is minimal noise on them, but the number of details is very high. Typical flagship level. Another innovation is the Center Stage front camera sensor.  Its resolution has increased from 12 to 18 megapixels, but most importantly, it is now square and can be used to shoot in both vertical and horizontal modes.  The trick here is that now you no longer need to turn your phone over, even if you are filming with a group.  There's enough room in the frame for everyone.  And thanks to artificial intelligence, the smartphone will automatically suggest the optimal viewing angle.  In general, the front camera has become really more interesting. As before, video can be shot in 4K with a frame rate of up to 120 frames per second. This is a big plus because it's easy to slow down a video like this in post to get those smooth, cinematic shots. However, even without slowdown, the stabilization works great, smoothing out almost all vibrations and tremors.  This smartphone allows you to shoot handheld without the need for external stabilizers.  And this is, of course, very cool.  In general, video recording has always been a strong point of iPhones and a special emphasis has always been placed on it.  For example, we have access to a logarithmic profile with the Apple Pro Ress codec.  This makes it possible to make deeper and more professional color correction.  However, be prepared that 1 minute of such a video will take up about a gigabyte of memory. So for video shooting, I recommend getting a model with at least 512 GB, and preferably more.  This way you won't have to constantly think about how much free memory you have left.  Let's move on to hardware.  And here too there are certain changes.  The new product received an Apple A19 Pro chip with an increased core clock frequency.  Processor performance has not increased much compared to the A 18 Pro. In synthetic tests the difference is only 10-15%. But the video core has become much more powerful.  And here the difference in some tests can reach 30%.  So in a sense, the new iPhone is also a great gaming smartphone.  Moreover, it is also suitable for gaming because it has a new cooling system.  The fact is that this is the first smartphone from Apple equipped with a special vapor chamber.  It is located right in this cutout on the back of the smartphone.  This thing is needed to remove and dissipate heat from the components.  Inside the chamber there is deionized water, hermetically sealed in a metal case. The principle of operation here is quite simple.  When heated, the water evaporates, moving to another part of the chamber.  There it condenses again, giving off heat, and then the cycle repeats over and over again. This way, excess heat is evenly dissipated throughout the case, reducing component heating.  In practice, this means that the smartphone does n't heat up as much, and the heat isn't concentrated in any particular area, but is distributed evenly throughout the entire body.  It seemed to me that the area around the camera was slightly warmer than the bottom of the smartphone, but overall there was almost no difference in temperature. Nothing gets very hot anywhere, the case just gets slightly warm.  And even when you play for several hours in a row, the situation remains stable.  The processor does not throttle, and the screen brightness does not decrease. I also had no issues with the quality of the cellular network signal reception , despite the fact that the new Apple C1X modem was not installed here. The device has good network reception.  But there is progress with fast charging.  With a powerful power supply that supports Power Delivery, the new smartphone charges slightly faster than before.  For example, my 65W UGE charger fully charged the battery in 1 hour and 15 minutes.  Moreover, the first 90% is collected in just an hour.  This is a very decent indicator for Apple products.  So, what conclusion can be drawn from all of the above ?  Let's start with the fact that from a technical point of view, the new iPhone did not receive any revolutionary changes.  Yes, they've slightly improved the telephoto lens, as always, improved the processor, and slightly improved the battery life and cooling system, but there are no revolutionary changes in the hardware itself.  But visually, the new product has been significantly redesigned.  And this is perhaps the most significant update since the iPhone 12. Apple has completely reimagined the device's appearance, setting a new direction for its development over the next 3-4 years.  And I think that it is the seventeenth generation of iPhones that will largely determine what the flagships of the coming years will look like, and not only from Apple.  Well, now the main question: is it worth upgrading at all?  In my opinion, if you use the fifteenth, let alone the sixteenth generation of Pros, you won’t notice a big difference in everyday tasks.  In this case, upgrading to a new smartphone is only worth it if you value a fresh design or just want something new.  Well, if you're using the twelfth, thirteenth, or fourteenth generation, you can confidently upgrade, since Apple has really improved its smartphones over the years.  In any case, the iPhone 17 Pro is still one of the most ultimate compact flagships , which is perfect for both work and entertainment. Finally, I'd like to remind you that new Apple products are now available for purchase at Setinka with a store warranty.  There is also a huge selection of computers, laptops, televisions, home and kitchen appliances, and other gadgets for all occasions.  All the products there are original and come with an official warranty, so I have been buying electronics there for many years.  The link will be, as usual, in the description.  Go ahead, take a look and choose something interesting for yourself .  Well, we'll see you in the next issues.  Until next time.

title: Onko Apple pilannut iPhonen salaman? 💡📱 Testissä iPhone 17 Pro Max 😮
transcript: Has Apple messed up the iPhone and Lightning?  In the new iPhone 17 Pro and Pro Max phones, the flash has been moved from next to the lens to the other side of the phone, and this is said to cause really bad things to photos and videos. Well, this is where the difference really stands out, especially when the phone is moving. So if you often take flash photos or videos in dimly lit spaces, such as restaurants, then the iPhone 17 Pro is not a good choice.  But note that this problem only occurs on Pro devices, not on the standard 17 or iPhones.  M.

title: Apple iPhone 17 Pro Max Production Cost تكلفة انتاج ايفون ١٧ برو ماكس
transcript: How much do you think Apple costs to make the iPhone 17 Pro Max ?  Let me tell you, the aluminum body costs $20, and the battery costs $4. Imagine if they spent $10 on the battery, how much battery would that be? The 256GB storage costs $20, the RAM costs $21, and the modem that handles Wi-Fi and 4G is $90. The iPhone screen costs $80, the processor or chip is $90, and the cameras are $80. So, the entire iPhone 17 Pro Max costs Apple $408. Apple sells this phone for $1200, meaning they make a 200% profit on just one phone.

title: iPhone 17 Pro Review - Հայերեն
transcript: Hello everyone, I'll do anything for you. Welcome to my channel.  This year, Apple introduced four new iPhones: the 17, 17 Pro, 17 Pro Max, and Air.  You are now watching the iPhone 17 Pro show.  Air's video is already ready and if you're interested, you can watch it after this video. This year, as in previous years, I will compare the new iPhones with last year's iPhone 16 Pro.  The first thing that stands out is , of course, the design. Apple originally made its new models out of stainless steel and used it for many years.  Until we saw Titan in the iPhone 15 Pro and 16 Pro .  It's a more expensive, lighter, and stronger metal, but it seems like for some reason Apple decided to use aluminum for its new 17 Pro. Of course, one of those reasons is aluminum's ability to dissipate heat, but in my opinion, there were also issues of cost reduction here .  Anyway, the aluminum really helps keep the phone from getting too hot.  Plus, for the first time, Apple has installed a Vaper Chamber, which better dissipates heat.  This, if you know, has been used for many years in other Android smartphones, as well as in computers for passive cooling.   I should also mention that aluminum is more pleasant to use and feels softer in the hand , but be careful, if you drop it on the ground, it is easier to see the dents and damage. Like the iPhone Air, Apple has moved its components to this top plate in this model.  Yes, that's what Apple calls this raised or protruding part.  This allowed for a larger battery or battery, as well as added antennas along these raised edges, which improved 4G or 5G internet and cellular coverage in general .  I don't know what you think of this design, but for example, I often shoot videos or tilt the phone and use it with two hands.  It's not very comfortable to hold, for example, my left hand completely covers either the light or this depth sensor, but what I definitely didn't get used to after almost two weeks of using it was the new bright orange color.  If you don't put it in a case, the color is definitely visible from the front and I just don't like the orange or even white edges.  And that's why I use blue.   The Pros come in blue, silver, and orange, and unfortunately there's no black color. The sizes start at 256 gigabytes and even go up to 2 Pro Max models. For example, in America and Canada and several other countries, the iPhone 17 Pro and Pro Max do not have a SIM card slot. Last year there was no SIM card slot either, but this time they have a larger battery to make up for the lack of space for the SIM card.  If you want a model with a SIM card, you can buy it from Europe or England .  Compared to last year's 16 Pro , I didn't feel any difference in the dynamics.  The sound quality is very good and as you can hear for yourself now, let's move on to the screen.  In reality, not much has changed, but there are some changes. The screen is the same, but it seems brighter, has some anti-reflective coating, and is harder to scratch, but I haven't noticed all of these myself, to be honest .  Katube, who tests the screen hardness of iPhones and other smartphones, confirmed that the screen is indeed much harder to scratch than last year's 16 Pro.  Before moving on to cameras and pictures, let me briefly summarize the remaining changes.   The iPhone 17 Pro has a nearly 20% larger battery than last year.  This will easily last you a day or even more.  It has 12 gigabytes instead of last year's eight, a little faster charging both wirelessly and wired, and of course a slightly newer and faster chip A19 Pro compared to last year, but I don't think you'll notice any significant difference, that is, a significant difference with last year's 16 pro is mainly visible in its appearance, that is, in the design and yes, a little longer life, but if the rest of the things I listed don't appeal to you that much, then you simply won't see much difference compared to last year, apart from the design. And now let's move on to the pictures. Pro models, as you know, have three cameras: main, wide-angle, and zoom.  The main and wide- angle cameras remain the same at 48 megapixels, but this year the zoom camera is now 48 megapixels in the 17-megapixel range .  But this time, I don't know why it's only zooming in four times. Last year it was five times, but last year I should also remind you that those five times it was taking 12-megapixel pictures. I've taken quite a few pictures, both with the 16, with the 7, and from comparing the overall pictures, I can say that the biggest change in this year's new 17 Pro is the colors.  They look more accurate to my eyes than they did in last year's 16 Pro.  The 16 Pro is on the right in all these pictures and the 17 Pro, the new one, is on the left. Last year's model has more artificial, brighter, and more visible or luminous colors.  In this very picture, I can tell that the real image was the one taken by the 17 Pro.  It was more true when I took the picture at that moment.  Here, for example, the new model shows more accurately the color of the asphalt and the shadow of the building from the sun, which is more pronounced.  Of course, there are cases where the difference is almost imperceptible. Color accuracy also extends to a zoom camera, as in this case.  By the way, the zoom camera doesn't make that much of a difference in the pictures.  For example, I don't know which one you like better in these two pictures, but as soon as the light starts to get bad, the new model cleans up the picture better and shows the true lighting.   I can say the same thing for regular pictures in bad, very bad lighting .  Look, for example, at how well the cross of the place is visible and at the same time how bright the colored parts of the window are. With this picture, you can understand that the new iPhone really tries to show the color correctly. Of course, in some pictures it doesn't show, so this is not a definite thing that you see in every picture. I also noticed that right after taking a picture in night mode, the new iPhone doesn't seem to want to open the picture too much, which I think is good. For example, in this picture, the green color of the tree looks more accurate to me, as if in the new model.  Finally, I should also mention that there is a macro or wide-angle camera, which has also received color improvements.  Although I don't see any improvement in the pictures in poor lighting.   That's about it for the rear cameras, but of course there's also a new front-facing selfie camera, which, like all new iPhones this year, has a square sensor.  This means that it will allow you to take selfies in a vertical format, or for example, you can use the camera in front of you as a camera for some online meeting instead of the laptop camera.  If we're talking about shooting videos, I think it's needless to mention again that Apple simply can't catch up when comparing videos shot on an iPhone to other smartphones.  There is simply no other smartphone that has as much technology as the 17 Pro or Pro Max. The closest thing that tries to catch up to the iPhone is probably the Galaxy S25 Ultra from Samsung, which I think has the only advantage over the iPhone, which is that it has the ability to shoot 8K video .  The strangest thing is that Apple can actually allow us to take pictures with our iPhones, for that you just need a minimum of a 35 megapixel camera, which these smartphones have, but I don't understand why they don't add more. Anyway, I can't say much about video, just know that the iPhone has the best video camera in any smartphone today. And that's how this program ends. What can I tell you to sum it up?   The iPhone 17 Pro is a truly successful model. Unfortunately, we lost the blue, which I really liked in the iPhone 15 Pro and 16 Pro models, as well as the absence of black and natural titanium colors.  You don't know why there's no black this year, but at the same time we get improvements in this model with its heat-cooling capability, longer life, a new selfie camera, no price increase, and a new iPhone with models starting at 256.  That's important.  Maybe if you're coming from an iPhone 16 Pro , you won't feel the changes as much in the 17 Pro , because last year, for example, there was a real difference in the battery and phone life improvements from the 15 Pro to the 16.  Design aside, you 'll really feel the difference in everyday use on the 17 Pro if you have an older model, say a 15 Pro or older, and in this regard, I'd definitely recommend upgrading to the 17 Pro.  If you have an older model than the iPhone 15 Pro, I would also recommend buying last year's iPhone 16 Pro. If Titan, for example, is more important to you , since we have already switched to aluminum in the 17 Pro, and if you don't want to spend more money and the 16 Pro will completely satisfy you for years. Thank you for watching this very long- prepared program. To be honest, I have n't been feeling well for about a month, which is why the preparation of the video was delayed, but now everything is fine. Thank you very much for watching my programs and waiting for the new ones. Mika was with you. We will meet in the near future.
